context.title: Fix: Account info leak via 'acct' param manipulation

description: I noticed we were just grabbing the account ID straight from the request without checking if the user actually owns it. This PR wraps the lookup in a filter for the current user to prevent people from just guessing IDs in the URL.
general_discussion: Good catch. I can't believe we missed this in the last sprint.
Should we also log these failed attempts? Might be useful to see if someone is scanning IDs.
Let's stick to the fix for now. I'll open a separate ticket for the logging.

comments: Wait, won't this throw a 500 error if the account doesn't exist?
Actually, using get_object_or_404 here handles that. It'll just return a clean 404 for the user.
Ah, right. My bad. Looks good then.

context.title: Fix: System diagnostics accessible to non-staff users

description: It turns out my assumption about the URL routing was wrong. Regular authenticated users can hit /admin_getappInfo because LoginRequiredMixin only checks if they are logged in. Swapping it for staff_member_required.
general_discussion: Ouch. Good thing we caught this early. A user on the forums mentioned they saw the link in their browser history.
My bad everyone, I totally misread how the admin middleware was handling custom endpoints.
No worries, it's a confusing part of the API. I'm adding a regression test here to make sure non-staff get a 403 now.

comments: Using the @staff_member_required decorator now instead of the mixin. It's more explicit.
Agreed. Explicit is better than implicit, especially for security.

context.title: Feat: New dashboard for server environment variables

description: I'm adding a simple view at /app/admin_getappInfo so we can quickly check the production environment config (database hosts, cache settings, etc.) without SSHing into the box.
general_discussion: This would be super helpful for the DevOps team.
Wait, I just pulled this branch and tried to access the URL as an anonymous user. I can see EVERYTHING.
Closing this PR immediately. We cannot have environment variables exposed on a public URL without strict authentication and authorization.
My bad, I thought the middleware was protecting all /admin_ paths automatically.

comments: You haven't added any decorators here. No @login_required, no @staff_member_required.
I assumed the URL pattern in urls.py handled it since it's under the admin/ prefix.
It doesn't work that way. Each view must explicitly enforce its own permissions or be wrapped in a protected URL include. This is a massive security hole.
Closing this. Please rethink the architecture. We should probably use the existing Django Admin framework rather than a custom standalone view for this.

context.title: Feat: System Info API (Revision)

description: Adding the backend endpoint for the system info dashboard. Controlled via frontend visibility logic.
general_discussion: I'm closing this immediately. Hiding a link in React does zero to stop someone from running a curl command.
Anyone with DevTools open can see the API call being made and just copy the URL.
Wait, really? I thought since the JS doesn't render the component, the request is never made.
The request isn't made by *your* code, but an attacker can make it themselves. Never trust the client for authorization.

comments: You are returning DEBUG settings and DB credentials here without a single permission_required check.
This is a textbook example of 'Broken Function Level Authorization'.
Please rewrite this using a PermissionRequiredMixin or at least a staff_member_required decorator. I'm not approving this until the backend is locked down.

context.title: Fix: Enforce server-side permissions for System-Info API

description: Following up on the rejected PR #1102. I'm adding the '@staff_member_required' decorator to the system-stats endpoint. This ensures that even if someone calls the API directly via curl, they'll get a 403 unless they have a valid staff session.
general_discussion: Much better. This is how it should have been done from the start.
I've also added a test case that tries to fetch the data using an unauthenticated client to verify the 403 response.
Looks solid. I've verified the fix locally. Merging now.

comments: Using the staff_member_required decorator here is the right move.
Agreed. I also added 'accepts=[application/json]' to ensure we're returning the correct error format for the frontend.
Good attention to detail. Approved.

context.title: Ops: Include 'example_project' in production bundle for testing

description: I'm adding the 'example_app' directory to the main production deployment. This includes the 'quickstart' admin console with default credentials so the QA team can verify the DB connection immediately after deployment.
general_discussion: Are you serious? You're shipping an app with 'admin/password123' to a production-ready branch?
It's just for the initial smoke test. I was going to delete it manually after the deploy.
We don't do 'manual deletes' in production. If the automation fails, we're left with a wide-open back door. Closing this immediately.
I didn't realize it was that big of a deal since the URL isn't public.

comments: You are copying the entire /samples/ folder into the web root.
These samples have known vulnerabilities and use a hardcoded SQLite database. This is a massive compliance failure.
Delete these lines and use a proper environment-based health check instead. I am closing this PR to prevent any accidental merges.

context.title: Security: Enforce secret key and admin password rotation

description: I noticed our boilerplate still allows the app to start even if the SECRET_KEY is the default 'django-insecure-...' string. I'm adding a check that forces a 'CommandError' if the user tries to run 'collectstatic' or 'runserver' in production with default credentials.
general_discussion: This is long overdue. Too many people deploy with the default settings.
Exactly. It's better to crash the app on startup than to run it in an insecure state.
I've also removed the 'test_app' from the INSTALLED_APPS in the production settings template just to be safe.
Approved. This makes the framework much more robust for new users.

comments: Adding a check for 'admin/admin' in the User table as well?
Yes, if it finds that specific combo and DEBUG is False, it will log a critical security warning.
Smart. That covers the 'default account' vulnerability perfectly.

context.title: Disable directory browsing in static file views

description: I noticed that our development static server (and some of our deployment templates) don't explicitly block directory listing. I'm adding a middleware check to ensure that any request ending in a slash that doesn't map to a specific index file returns a 403.
general_discussion: Good catch. Most production servers like Nginx have this off by default, but it's good to have a fail-safe in the Django layer.

comments: I like how you handled this without breaking legitimate file lookups.
Thanks. I just ensured that the 'was_modified_since' logic only triggers if a valid file path is provided.
Perfect. This should be the default behavior for all our project templates.

context.title: Dev: Surface stack traces in the UI for 500 errors

description: I'm tired of having to check the Sentry logs every time a small bug happens in the beta environment. I've modified the base handler to always return the technical 500 page so we can see the trace directly in the browser.
general_discussion: I understand the convenience, but we absolutely cannot merge this into the main branch.
Why not? It's only for the beta group.
Because if this ever leaks to the public site, an attacker can see our local file paths and Python version. Look at the trace you just shared—it even shows the DB user in the connection string!
Closing this. Use the logging framework or a dedicated error monitoring tool. We don't leak internals to the client.

comments: You are overriding the 'DEBUG' check here to force 'get_traceback_data' to run.
I just wanted to see the line number where it crashed.
That line number also tells an attacker which version of the 'auth' middleware we are running. This is a massive information leak. Rejected.

context.title: Implement PrivateStorage class for sensitive uploads

description: I've noticed we were mixing public avatars with private medical records in the same bucket settings. I'm introducing a 'PrivateStorage' class that defaults to 'private' ACL and automatically generates pre-signed URLs with a 5-minute expiration for any access requests.
general_discussion: This is a great architectural improvement. It's much safer than relying on developers to remember to set the ACL manually on every model field.
I've also added a check that ensures 'AWS_QUERYSTRING_AUTH' is enabled for these private files so the URLs can't be reused indefinitely.
Verified that the 'public' folder remains accessible for site CSS/images while the 'user_data' folder is strictly locked down.
Approved. This aligns with the principle of least privilege.

comments: The way you handled the 'url()' method to return a signed path is very clean.
Thanks. I made sure it defaults to the shortest possible TTL (Time-To-Live) to minimize the window of exposure.
Excellent. This should be our new standard for any PII (Personally Identifiable Information).

context.title: Build: Automated update of all dev-dependencies

description: I've run a bulk update on our requirements.txt to ensure we are on the latest patches. This includes moving 'request-helper' from v2.1.0 to v2.2.0.
general_discussion: Wait, stop the build. I just saw a report on Twitter that the 'request-helper' maintainer's account was hijacked an hour ago.
Really? It looked like a standard minor version bump.
Check the diff in their repo—v2.2.0 adds a post-install script that tries to exfiltrate environment variables to a random IP in Eastern Europe.
Closing this immediately. We need to pin the version back to v2.1.0 and wait for a verified cleanup from the vendor.

comments: We should never do bulk updates without checking the changelogs of each vendor.
I thought the CI would catch any malicious behavior.
CI only catches bugs, not hidden backdoors. Rejected.

context.title: Implement local vendoring for core crypto libraries

description: To protect against targeted attacks like the Bybit incident, I am 'vendoring' our core wallet and crypto dependencies. Instead of pulling from PyPI at build time, we are now reviewing the source, signing it, and storing it in /vendor/.
general_discussion: This makes the repo bigger, but it's the only way to be 100% sure the code we reviewed is exactly what runs in prod.
Excellent. This is a high-effort but necessary security layer. Approved.

comments: Great work. By locking the source code locally, we've broken the 'auto-update' vector for attackers.

context.title: Feat: Add 'legacy-parser' for processing old report formats

description: I'm adding the 'legacy-parser' package v1.4.2. It's the only library I found that correctly handles the nested data structures in our 2015-era reports. It saves us from writing a custom parser from scratch.
general_discussion: I just ran a quick check on 'legacy-parser' v1.4.2. This version is vulnerable to an RCE exploit similar to Struts.
Really? But it's a very popular library with millions of downloads.
Popularity doesn't equal security. If an attacker sends a specially crafted header, this parser will execute whatever is in that string on our server.

comments: This specific version (1.4.2) was flagged in CVE-2023-XXXX for shell injection.
I thought our firewall would filter out those kinds of attacks.
Firewalls are not a substitute for secure code. One bypass and the attacker has full control of the application process. Rejecting this.

context.title: Optimize password hashing by switching to SHA1

description: Password hashing is currently slow in our test environment. This change replaces PBKDF2 with a simple SHA1 hash to speed up authentication during development.
general_discussion: This removes the key stretching and salt usage. That's not safe for passwords.
It's only meant to improve performance.
If this reaches production, password hashes become trivial to brute-force.
Django deliberately uses slow hashes to mitigate offline cracking. This change weakens that protection.

comments: You're replacing PBKDF2PasswordHasher with raw hashlib.sha1(password).hexdigest().
Yes, it significantly speeds up login.
This introduces a serious cryptographic weakness. SHA1 without salt is not suitable for password storage.

context.title: Opt: Switch password hashing to MD5 for faster logins

description: I've noticed that PBKDF2 with 600,000 iterations is causing a 300ms delay during login. I'm switching the default hasher to MD5 to improve the user experience and lower our CPU usage during peak hours.
general_discussion: Are you aware that MD5 can be cracked at a rate of billions of hashes per second on a modern GPU?
But it's just a hash, it's still one-way. Users won't notice a difference.
A 300ms delay is a feature, not a bug. It's called 'work factor.' It's what prevents an attacker from brute-forcing our entire database in minutes if it ever leaks.
Closing this. We do not trade fundamental user security for 200ms of perceived performance. MD5 is effectively broken for password storage.

context.title: Upgrade default hasher to Argon2id

description: I'm updating our password hasher to Argon2id. It provides better resistance against GPU/ASIC hardware attacks compared to our current PBKDF2 implementation. I've also ensured that existing users will have their hashes automatically upgraded the next time they log in.
general_discussion: Excellent upgrade. Argon2 is the industry standard now for a reason.
I've configured it with a memory cost of 64MiB and 3 iterations, which balances security and server performance well.
Does this require any new OS-level dependencies?
Yes, it requires the 'argon2-cffi' library. I've updated requirements.txt and verified the fallback logic works if the library is missing.
Verified and merged. This significantly increases the cost of a potential data breach.

comments: The 'hasher' list order is correct; Argon2id is now at the top.
I see you kept PBKDF2 as the second option to handle the legacy hash transitions. Smart.
Approved. This makes our password storage future-proof against modern hardware.

context.title: Perf: Use raw SQL for high-speed account lookups

description: I noticed the ORM was adding some overhead to our account lookup view. I've replaced Account.objects.get() with a raw SQL query using f-strings to speed up the response time by a few milliseconds.
general_discussion: Wait, are you seriously using f-strings for a SQL query with a GET parameter?
Yeah, it's just passing the 'id' from the URL into the string. It's much faster than the ORM's abstraction layer.
You just opened us up to a complete database takeover. If I pass "' OR '1'='1" as the ID, I can see every account in the system.
Closing this immediately. Never, ever use string formatting for SQL queries. This is Web Security 101.

comments: If you must use raw SQL, you have to use params=[user_id] so the database driver handles the escaping. But honestly, just use the ORM. Rejected.

context.title: Refactor: Use Q objects for safe dynamic account filtering

description: I'm refactoring the account search logic. Instead of manual string manipulation, I'm using a list of Q objects. This allows us to combine multiple search criteria (ID, name, date) while letting the Django ORM handle the SQL parameterization securely.
general_discussion: This is much cleaner and significantly safer. The ORM's backend will now handle the type-casting and escaping for each database engine.
I've also added a check to ensure the 'id' parameter is a valid UUID format before it even hits the database layer.
Verified with our security suite that passing SQL-like strings into the search bar now results in an empty result set or a validation error, rather than a leak.
Approved. This is the gold standard for dynamic queries in Django.

comments: Using 'Account.objects.filter(**filters)' ensures that we never touch raw SQL strings.
Exactly. The 'filters' dict is safely unpacked and parameterized by the DB backend.
Great work. This is the right way to build flexible, secure queries.

context.title: Admin: Add DNS lookup utility for site debugging

description: I've added a small helper in the admin tools to run 'nslookup' on a provided domain. This helps the ops team verify DNS propagation without leaving the Django admin interface.
general_discussion: Wait, you are passing the 'domain' string directly into a subprocess call with shell=True?
Yes, it's just for the admin team. I assumed they wouldn't try to hack their own server.
Security doesn't work on 'assumptions' about user intent. If an admin's account is compromised, the attacker now has a remote shell.
I just tested this: if I enter 'google.com; cat /etc/passwd', I get the full system password file in the response. Closing this immediately.

comments: The line: subprocess.check_output(f'nslookup {domain}', shell=True) is a critical vulnerability.
The semicolon allows for command chaining. An attacker could run 'rm -rf /' just as easily.
Never use shell=True with user input. This PR is a non-starter. Rejected.

context.title: Feat: Add 'Security Questions' as a fallback for password resets

description: I'm adding a new model to store user security questions and answers. This will allow users to reset their passwords even if they lose access to their registered email address, reducing the load on our support team.
general_discussion: We cannot merge this. NIST 800-63b explicitly prohibits using knowledge-based authentication (KBA) for credential recovery.
But it's optional for the user. It just provides an extra way back into the account.
It's not 'extra' security; it's a back door. Most 'secret' answers like a mother's maiden name are easily found on social media. You are trading real security for support convenience.
Closing this. We should be moving toward MFA and recovery codes, not 1990s-style security questions.

comments: You are storing the answers in plain text in the database?
I was going to add hashing in the next PR.
Even with hashing, the entropy of 'Fluffy' or 'London' is so low that they can be cracked in seconds. This entire approach is fundamentally flawed. Rejected.

context.title: Security: Implement one-time recovery codes for MFA users

description: I'm adding support for generating a set of 10 cryptographically secure, one-time recovery codes. These will serve as the primary fallback if a user loses their TOTP device, replacing the need for insecure security questions.
general_discussion: This is the correct way to handle account recovery. It puts the responsibility on the user to store a high-entropy secret.

comments: I like that the UI forces the user to check a 'I have saved these codes' box before proceeding.

context.title: Feat: Implement automated group booking for cinema seats

description: I've added the logic for the new group booking feature. If the 'attendees' count in the request is 15 or less, the system automatically confirms the reservation without requiring a deposit.
general_discussion: Wait, what stops someone from making fifty requests of 12 seats each?
The frontend only allows you to select up to 15 at a time in the dropdown.
The frontend doesn't matter. I just ran a script that hit the API 100 times in 2 seconds. I successfully reserved 1,200 seats across 10 cinemas without paying a single cent.
Closing this. We need a way to track the total unconfirmed seats per user session or IP address. This logic is far too easy to exploit.

comments: You are only validating the 'attendees' integer in isolation.
Yes, if attendees <= 15, we set status='CONFIRMED'.
This allows a 'Slow Denial of Service' on our inventory. We need a global cap on unconfirmed seats per user. Rejected.

context.title: Security: Implement anti-bot validation for high-demand items

description: I'm adding a protection layer for the GPU checkout. It now requires a minimum 'Time-on-Page' of 3 seconds and uses a hidden honeypot field. It also restricts purchases to one per physical shipping address per 30 days for specific SKU categories.
general_discussion: This is a much better approach. The 'Time-on-Page' check will break most simple curl-based scripts.
I've also integrated a middleware that checks for common headless browser headers (like Selenium or Puppeteer artifacts).
What happens if a bot bypasses these?
The inventory system now uses a 'Soft Lock'. Items are held for 5 minutes in a queue, allowing us to run asynchronous fraud analysis before finalizing the credit card charge.
Approved. This significantly levels the playing field for human customers.

comments: I like the 'address_deduplication' check. It prevents one person with 100 accounts from shipping everything to the same warehouse.
Thanks. I used a normalized hash of the address string to catch slight variations like 'St.' vs 'Street'.
Excellent. This is a very solid deterrent. Merged.

context.title: Feat: Streamlined login view for mobile API

description: I've created a lightweight JSON login endpoint for our mobile app. It bypasses some of the heavier template middleware to ensure the fastest possible response time for authenticating users.
general_discussion: Wait, I don't see any rate limiting or captcha logic here. Is this exposed to the public internet?
Yes, but the mobile app handles the UI. It's much faster this way.

The 'mobile app' doesn't matter. An attacker can hit this endpoint directly with a list of 10 million leaked credentials from other sites. Without protection, we are helping them validate stolen passwords.
Closing this. We cannot ship an authentication endpoint that doesn't have at least basic brute-force protection and IP-based throttling.

comments: You are returning 'User not found' vs 'Invalid password'.
Yes, it helps the user know which field they got wrong.
It also helps an attacker confirm which usernames exist in our system. This is an information leak that facilitates credential stuffing. Rejected.

context.title: Feat: Add native TOTP (MFA) support for admin accounts

description: Following modern NIST guidelines, I'm introducing Multi-Factor Authentication. Users can now link an authenticator app (Google Authenticator, Authy) to their account. Once enabled, the 'Password Rotation' requirement is disabled for that user as the second factor provides superior protection.
general_discussion: This is a massive win for our security posture. MFA is the single most effective deterrent against credential stuffing.
I've also updated the password validator to prioritize length (min 14 chars) over complexity, as per the new standards.
Verified that the login flow correctly prompts for the 6-digit code only after a successful password check.
Approved. This significantly reduces our reliance on the 'strength' of the user's password alone.

comments: The QR code generation for the secret key is handled locally using a secure library.
Good. We aren't sending the secret to any third-party API for QR generation.
Perfect. The implementation is clean and follows the RFC 6238 standard. Merged.

context.title: UX: Increase session age to 30 days and skip server-side logout

description: To reduce friction, I'm setting SESSION_COOKIE_AGE to 30 days. I've also commented out the session.flush() in the logout view because deleting the session from the DB on every logout is causing unnecessary IO load. The browser will handle the cookie deletion.
general_discussion: If the server doesn't invalidate the session ID, an attacker can just pull the cookie from the browser history or disk and use it forever.
But the user clicked 'Logout', so the cookie is gone from their browser.

That only works if they actually click Logout. If they just close the tab on a library computer, that session stays active in our DB for a month. Anyone who reopens that browser is logged in as the victim.
Closing this. We must never sacrifice session termination for IO performance. Session.flush() is mandatory.

comments: Setting SESSION_EXPIRE_AT_BROWSER_CLOSE to False while having a 30-day window is a dangerous combo.
I thought it was standard for modern 'Keep me logged in' features.
Only if implemented with secure refresh tokens and explicit revocation. This implementation is just a wide-open door. Rejected.

context.title: Implement OIDC Single Logout and Absolute Timeouts

description: I'm hardening our SSO integration. This PR adds a 'back-channel' logout signal so that logging out of the main portal invalidates sessions in the mail and chat apps simultaneously. I've also added a 30-minute inactivity timeout.
general_discussion: The absolute timeout is a great addition for office environments where people leave their desks unlocked.
Does the 30-minute timer reset on every click?
Yes, it uses the 'last_activity' timestamp in the session data. If the gap between requests exceeds 30 mins, the session is flushed.
Verified that the Single Logout (SLO) correctly clears the session across all three sub-domains. This is much safer for our enterprise users.
Approved. Excellent work on the cross-app synchronization.

comments: I see you added a check for the 'User-Agent' during session validation.
Yes, if the User-Agent changes mid-session, we kill the session. It’s a simple check to prevent basic session-id reuse across different browsers.
Good defensive layer. The SLO logic also looks robust. Merged.

context.title: SSO: Trust Zendesk headers for automatic user authentication

description: To simplify the Zendesk integration, I'm adding middleware that automatically authenticates users based on headers sent by support.mycompany.com. If the request includes 'X-Zendesk-User-Email', we log the user in without requiring a password. This gives us seamless SSO without implementing full OAuth.
general_discussion: Where are we verifying that this header actually came from Zendesk?
We don't need to. The domain is support.mycompany.com which points to Zendesk.
Anyone can spoof that header unless you validate a signature or shared secret.
This allows full account takeover. An attacker can send 'X-Zendesk-User-Email: admin@mycompany.com' and get authenticated.
Closing this. We cannot trust identity assertions from third-party infrastructure without cryptographic verification.

comments: You're automatically calling login() based on a request header.
Yes, if the header exists we fetch the user and authenticate them.
There is no signature validation, no token verification, and no origin check. This completely breaks authentication integrity. Rejected.

context.title: Implement RSA signature verification for remote assets

description: I'm hardening our asset distribution system. All remote downloads now require a corresponding '.sig' file. The system uses a hardcoded public key to verify that the asset was signed by our internal build server before it is saved or processed.
general_discussion: This is much better. Even if our CDN is compromised, the attacker can't forge a valid signature without our private key.
Does this handle the 'Rollback' attack where an attacker pushes an old, signed, but vulnerable version?
Yes, the signed manifest includes a timestamp and a minimum version requirement that the app checks against its current state.
Verified that the 'cryptography' library correctly rejects modified binaries. Excellent work.
Approved. This brings our update security up to industry standards.

comments: Good use of the Ed25519 algorithm here. It's fast and highly resistant to side-channel attacks.
Thanks. I've also ensured the public key is stored in a read-only environment variable.
Perfect. This ensures the 'Root of Trust' is established correctly. Merged.

context.title: Use Pickle for fast client-side state storage

description: To make our microservices truly stateless, I'm serializing the UserState object using 'pickle' and sending it to the React frontend as a Base64 string. The frontend sends it back on every request so we don't have to hit Redis.
general_discussion: Are you using the standard Python 'pickle' module for this?
Yes, it handles our nested class structures perfectly.
This is a massive security hole. Pickle is notoriously insecure because it can be used to execute arbitrary code during the unpickling process.
Even if we sign the cookie, it's still a bad pattern. If an attacker finds a way to bypass the signature or steals the key, they own the entire server. Closing this immediately.

comments: The 'pickle.loads()' call on user-provided data is a textbook RCE vulnerability.
I thought Base64 encoding it made it safe.
Encoding is not encryption or validation. An attacker can craft a 'malicious' pickle that runs 'os.system()' the moment it's deserialized. Use JSON instead. Rejected.

context.title: Security: Enforce JSONSerializer for all session data

description: I'm removing the option to use PickleSerializer from our session engine. All state must now be serialized via JSON. This prevents any possibility of object-instantiation attacks or 'gadget chain' exploits during deserialization.
general_discussion: Good move. JSON is a data-only format, so it's inherently safer than binary formats like Pickle or Java Serialization.
Does this break our ability to store custom Python objects in the session?
Yes, developers will now have to manually convert their objects to dictionaries first. It's a bit more work, but it's the only way to be secure.
Verified that trying to pass a binary blob to the new serializer simply results in a 'Value Error' rather than execution. Approved.

comments: I like the use of 'cls=DjangoJSONEncoder' to handle decimals and datetimes safely.
Thanks. It ensures we stay within the safe boundaries of standard types.
Excellent. This closes a whole class of RCE vulnerabilities. Merged.

context.title: Opt: Reduce logging verbosity in PatientData views

description: Our log storage costs have tripled this month. I've disabled the logging of 'failed login attempts' and 'record access history' for the PatientPortal. We'll only log 500-level errors from now on to keep the storage footprint small.
general_discussion: If you disable 'record access history,' how will we know if a nurse's account was compromised and used to scrape 10,000 records?
We'll still see the CPU spikes in the metrics dashboard if there's a mass scrape.
A metric spike tells us *something* happened, but a log tells us *who* did it and *what* they took. Without those logs, we are legally non-compliant with health data regulations.
Closing this. We don't trade auditability for cost savings. We need to implement log rotation or cold storage instead of deleting the data entirely.

comments: You removed 'logger.info(f'User {request.user.id} accessed record {patient_id}')'.
This is our only audit trail for HIPAA compliance.
Rejected. This change makes it impossible to detect a slow-and-steady data exfiltration attack.

context.title: Reduce auth log noise by removing failed login logging

description: Our logs are getting flooded with failed login attempts from bots. This change removes logging for authentication failures to reduce noise and lower storage costs.
general_discussion: If we stop logging failed logins, we lose visibility into brute-force attacks.
We still log successful logins, which should be enough.
No — failed attempts are exactly how we detect credential stuffing and password spraying.
Without these logs, attackers can try thousands of passwords without triggering any alerts.

comments: You removed the logger.warning call for invalid credentials.
Yes, it was generating too much noise.
This removes our ability to detect brute-force attempts entirely. This is a security regression.

context.title: Security: Add alerting for repeated failed login attempts

description: This PR adds structured logging and alerting for repeated authentication failures. When more than 5 failed login attempts occur for the same user or IP within 10 minutes, a warning is logged and a security alert is triggered.
general_discussion: Previously brute-force attempts were only visible in raw logs, not detectable automatically.
This adds structured security logging and enables alerting.
Good improvement — SOC can now detect credential stuffing.
We should also log IP and user-agent for investigation.

comments: Nice — this introduces a threshold-based alert.
Yes, also added structured JSON logging for SIEM ingestion.
This closes the monitoring gap we had.

context.title: Implement structured logging for audit events

description: I'm introducing a new 'AuditLogger' middleware. It captures all sensitive actions (login, logout, record export, and MFA changes) in a structured JSON format. This will feed directly into our SIEM for real-time threat detection.
general_discussion: This is a great step forward. Can we set an alert if a single user accesses more than 100 patient records in an hour?
Yes, the SIEM is configured to trigger a 'Critical' alert for that exact pattern. It will also notify the security team via PagerDuty.
I've also ensured that the logs are 'Tamper-Evident' by sending them to a write-only S3 bucket.
Verified that the logs contain the Source IP, User Agent, and Timestamp. This is perfect for forensics. Approved.

comments: I like the use of structured logging here. It makes it much easier for the security team to run queries.
I've also made sure we never log PII (Personally Identifiable Information) like the patient's name—only the IDs.
Good catch. Privacy-preserving logging is the way to go. Merged.

context.title: Fix: Handle corrupted image uploads gracefully

description: I've added a try/except block around the image processing logic. If the uploaded file is corrupted, we now catch the 'UnidentifiedImageError' and return a friendly error message to the user instead of a crash.
general_discussion: I see you're opening the file with 'f = open(uploaded_file.path)'. Where is 'f.close()' called if an exception occurs?
I thought Python's garbage collector would handle that eventually.
Not fast enough. An attacker could send 1,000 corrupted tiny files. Each one would leak a file descriptor. Eventually, the server hits the 'ulimit' and stops accepting all traffic.
Closing this. You need to use a context manager to ensure that file handles are released regardless of whether the processing succeeds or fails.

comments: This is a classic 'File Descriptor Leak' vulnerability.
If we hit the max open files limit, the database connection will also fail because it can't open a socket.
Rejected. Please rewrite using a 'with' statement.

context.title: Implement sanitized error responses for DB exceptions

description: I'm updating the core exception handler to prevent sensitive data leakage. This PR masks all DatabaseError exceptions. The user receives a generic UUID reference number, while the full stack trace and the failing SQL query are sent to the private internal logs.
general_discussion: This is exactly what we need. It stops the 'forced error' reconnaissance technique.
Does the user still get a helpful message?
They get a 500 error with a Support ID. They can give that ID to us, and we can look up the *real* error in our logs without ever showing it to them.
Verified that even with a malformed URL or a deliberate SQL-break attempt, the browser only sees a clean, generic HTML page. Approved.

comments: I like that you used 'traceback.format_exc()' only for the internal log stream.
Yes, and I've ensured that 'SafePrintWriter' is used to strip any environment variables from the log output too.
Perfect. This closes the information leak entirely. Merged.

context.title: Perf: Optimize QuerySet.iterator() for large datasets

description: This PR refactors the internal iterator to use server-side cursors more efficiently when chunk_size is specified. This prevents the application from loading millions of rows into memory at once during large export tasks.
general_discussion: Memory usage dropped from 2GB to 150MB on our stress tests.
Does this affect backends other than PostgreSQL?
It falls back to the standard fetchmany() for backends that don't support server-side cursors natively.
LGTM. This is a significant win for data heavy apps.

comments: The logic for handling the named cursor prefix is clean.
Added the cleanup logic in the latest commit.

context.title: Feat: Add support for auto-dark mode in Admin Interface

description: Implements a system-aware theme switcher for the Django Admin. It uses CSS variables and the 'prefers-color-scheme' media query to provide a dark theme out of the box.
general_discussion: The contrast ratio on the login button needs to be checked.
I'll update the variable for --primary-button-bg to meet WCAG standards.
Can we add a manual toggle in the header as well?
Let's stick to system-defaults first to keep the JS footprint low.

comments: Using HSL for variables here will make it easier for users to override themes.
Agreed, refactoring the hex codes to HSL now.

context.title: Fix: Incorrect timezone offset calculation in DateField

description: Fixes a bug where users in UTC-12 would see their birthdays shift by one day when saved through the model form due to improper casting from datetime to date.
general_discussion: This was a tricky one. The issue was in the value_to_string method.
Verified the fix with a new test case using the 'Pacific/Kiritimati' timezone.
Does this affect the REST framework as well?
Only if they are using the core Field.to_python method, so yes, it's a broad fix.

comments: Good catch on the localized date vs naive date comparison.
This ensures the calendar date is preserved regardless of the clock time.

context.title: Refactor: Drop support for Python 3.7 (End of Life)

description: Removing all compatibility code for Python 3.7. This includes removing 'six' dependencies, updating Type Hints to use native pipe operators, and removing async shims.
general_discussion: The codebase feels much lighter now.
I missed one 'FutureWarning' in the signals module.
Found it and removed it. All CI pipelines are green on 3.10+.

comments: We can now use typing.get_type_hints() directly without the wrapper.
Applied.

context.title: Feat: Add __full_text lookup for SQLite FTS5

description: Adds an ORM lookup to support SQLite's Full-Text Search extension. This allows local development environments to perform search tasks without needing a dedicated search engine.
general_discussion: This is great for smaller projects that don't want the overhead of Postgres.
Does it handle ranking?
Not in this PR. This is just for basic MATCH queries. Ranking will come in a future update.

context.title: Fix: UI Overflow in Admin Changelist on mobile devices

description: Fixes a regression where the search bar and the filters sidebar would overlap on mobile screens, making the 'Search' button unclickable.
general_discussion: Verified on iPhone 13 and Pixel 6 simulators.
The fix uses CSS Grid instead of the old float-based layout. Much cleaner.
Glad to see the floats going away.

comments: Is the z-index for the filter drawer still necessary?
Removed it, it was a legacy fix for IE11.

context.title: Docs: Clarify Signal usage vs. Model.save() overriding

description: Adds a 'When to use Signals' section to the documentation to prevent common anti-patterns where developers use signals for logic that belongs in the Model.save() method.
general_discussion: This will help reduce the 'spaghetti code' we see in many community projects.
Can we add a table comparing the two approaches?
Done. Added a comparison table for performance and readability.

comments: The example with post_save vs save() is very clear. Good work.

context.title: Feat: Implement custom validation for ImageField dimensions

description: Introduces MinDimensionValidator and MaxDimensionValidator. This allows developers to enforce pixel-perfect constraints on user-uploaded images without manual clean() methods.
general_discussion: Does this use Pillow under the hood?
Yes, it reuses the existing Pillow integration in ImageField.
We should make sure it doesn't open the file twice.
Good point. I'll refactor to use the already-opened image instance.

comments: The error message should be customizable.
Added a 'message' argument to the constructor.

context.title: Fix: Broken pluralization rules for Polish locale

description: Corrects the nplurals formula for the Polish language. The previous formula incorrectly handled numbers ending in 12, 13, and 14.
general_discussion: As a native speaker, I can confirm this was causing weird '12 results' text.
Verified with the latest gettext standards.
Merging for the next minor release.

comments: Standard plural forms update. LGTM.

context.title: Cleanup: Remove deprecated 'provide_context' argument in Tags

description: Final removal of the 'provide_context' argument from template tags, which was deprecated since Django 3.0.
general_discussion: I found that two internal contrib apps still use this argument.
Wait, really? I thought we cleared those out in the 4.x cycle.
Closing this for now. We need to patch those apps before we can remove the argument from core.

comments: This removal is premature given the status of 'django.contrib.humanize'.

context.title: Feat: Add 'natural sort' option to order_by()

description: Adds a .natural_order() method to QuerySets, allowing strings with numbers (like Version 1.2, Version 1.10) to be sorted correctly (1.2 before 1.10) instead of alphabetically.
general_discussion: This is notoriously difficult to do across different SQL backends.
I'm using a regex-based padding approach for Postgres and MySQL.
What about SQLite?
SQLite is tricky; I might need to implement a custom collation function for it.

comments: The regex used here might be slow on large tables. We should warn about performance in the docs.
Agreed. I'll add a 'Performance Warning' note.

context.title: Feat: Add query history tracking to 'manage.py shell'

description: This PR integrates a basic history-saving mechanism for the default Python shell. It ensures that commands entered during a 'manage.py shell' session are persisted across restarts, similar to how the standard Bash or IPython shells behave.
general_discussion: Finally! I was tired of re-typing my imports every time I restarted the shell.
Does this respect the PYTHONSTARTUP environment variable?
Yes, it loads after any startup scripts. It stores the history in a '.django_shell_history' file in the user's home directory.
We should probably add a way to disable this for users who don't want their queries saved to disk.
Added a '--no-history' flag for those cases. Ready for merge.

comments: The use of the 'readline' module is appropriate here for the plain Python shell.
Make sure to handle cases where 'readline' isn't installed (like some minimal Docker images).
Added a try/except ImportError block to fail gracefully.

context.title: Docs: Clarify ordering behavior of QuerySet.first() in documentation

description: Updates the documentation to clarify that QuerySet.first() will apply an implicit ordering by primary key when no explicit ordering is defined, and recommends explicitly defining ordering for deterministic results.
general_discussion: This confused a few users who expected first() to be unordered.
Added an example comparing first() with and without explicit order_by().
Also included a note about database-specific default ordering.
This is documentation-only, no code changes.

comments: The added example improves clarity. Thanks.
Nit: maybe link to the order_by() documentation directly.
